key_insight: In arsenene multilayers, interlayer bonding is not set by registry alone: thickness changes the coupling, driving a structural transition from wider-spaced few layers to compact bulk-like stacking and a semiconductor-to-semimetal band-gap collapse.
engineering_relevance: For 2D semiconductor design, layer count and stacking must be treated as coupled design variables because they strongly alter spacing, bonding, and electronic behavior.
method: Diffusion quantum Monte Carlo (DMC) used as the reference benchmark for interlayer separations and stacking energetics.
Density functional theory (DFT) compared multiple functionals against DMC, with SCAN+rVV10 performing best.
DMC-benchmarked SCAN+rVV10 used to predict thickness-dependent stacking evolution and electronic crossover.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that DMC finds bulk gray arsenic compact while few-layer structures remain much more separated, and that SCAN+rVV10 most closely reproduces the DMC equilibrium separations and energetics.
watch_item: The predicted crossover depends on accurate treatment of interlayer correlation and pz hybridization, so results may shift if the chosen functional or stacking model is not well matched to the real sample.
paper_title: Thickness-Dependent Interlayer Coupling and Semiconductor-to-Semimetal Crossover in Arsenene Multilayers
